metadata.seed: I mean, I'm a photographer, so I kind of. It's been stopping me a bit, my anxiety from being able to get work, and because every time there's an opportunity that is kind of involving me having to interact with people, I just tend to just turn it down. So I was kind of making things hard in that respect and just kind of friends wise and everything, I feel like I'm just kind of increasingly more alone. So it's kind of stopping me from making friends and, you know, like, relationships and stuff.

patient.query: I mean, I'm a photographer, so I kind of. It's been stopping me a bit, my anxiety from being able to get work, and because every time there's an opportunity that is kind of involving me having to interact with people, I just tend to just turn it down. So I was kind of making things hard in that respect and just kind of friends wise and everything, I feel like I'm just kind of increasingly more alone. So it's kind of stopping me from making friends and, you know, like, relationships and stuff.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not good enough to get work
I will be rejected by others

entailment: Fear of being in a small group (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like every chance to connect feels overwhelming, and you worry you might not be good enough or that people will reject you. That anxiety can make you pull back, which can leave you feeling more isolated. What might happen if you tried a small step toward a new connection, like a brief chat or a short project?

patient.query: Yeah, it's like, I want to connect, but then this voice in my head just… I don't know, it tells me I'm not interesting enough or that I’ll just embarrass myself. I guess I do pull back a lot because it feels safer, but, at the same time, I hate feeling alone. The idea of trying to chat with someone just makes my stomach turn.

entailment: Fear of being in a small group (finding)
neutral: Fear of speaking on the phone (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re torn between a strong desire to connect and a fear that you’ll be judged or embarrassed. That inner voice can feel very convincing, but it’s just one perspective among many. You might try noticing when it pops up and gently reminding yourself that you’re not alone in feeling this way. Small, manageable steps—like saying a quick hello—could help you test whether the fear holds up or fades.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being in a small group
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Automatic thoughts of not being interesting
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: I want to connect with people, but then this voice in my head tells me I’m not interesting enough or that I’ll embarrass myself. I pull back a lot because it feels safer, but I really hate feeling alone. Just the idea of chatting with someone makes my stomach turn.

entailment: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how much you’re craving connection yet feeling pulled back by that anxious voice. It can help to start with something very small—perhaps a quick text or a brief chat that feels low‑stakes—so you can test out the idea without the pressure of a full conversation. Remember, you’re not alone in feeling this way, and taking tiny steps can gradually ease that stomach‑turning fear.

patient.query: Yeah, I get what you're saying. I know that small steps might be easier, but even the thought of sending a quick text makes me anxious. It’s like I freeze up and start worrying about how the other person will respond. I don’t want to just say something stupid, you know? It feels like a lot, and I don't know if I can handle that yet.

automatic_thoughts: how will the other person respond?

entailment: Fear of speaking on the phone (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea of texting feels overwhelming, almost like a big hurdle. You’re not alone in worrying about how the other person will react or feeling that you might say something off. Maybe we could try drafting a quick message in a safe space first—just to see how it feels—before you actually send it. This could help you feel a bit more in control and less frozen.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: I hear you. I know small steps might help, but even texting someone makes me anxious. I freeze up and worry about how they’ll respond. I just don’t want to say something stupid. It feels so overwhelming, and I’m not sure I can handle that right now.

automatic_thoughts: I don't want to say something stupid
I can't handle that right now

entailment: Fear of speaking on the phone (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea of texting feels like a big hurdle right now. You might try starting with something very simple—just a quick question or a single word—to ease the pressure. If you feel the anxiety rising, pause, take a breath, and give yourself a moment before you hit send. Remember, it’s okay to feel overwhelmed; you can take it one small step at a time.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of speaking on the phone
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I appreciate that, but just thinking about doing something so simple still makes me feel really anxious. It’s like I can almost see the text on my screen, and my mind starts racing with all the things that could go wrong. I know it sounds silly, but it feels like a huge deal to me, and I don’t really know how to break that down. It’s frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: my mind starts racing with all the things that could go wrong
it feels like a huge deal

llm_response.response: It’s understandable that even a simple task can feel daunting when your mind keeps listing possible mishaps. That nervousness and frustration are common reactions when we worry about making mistakes. You might try to pause, focus on one small step, and notice how the anxiety shifts when you take it one piece at a time.

patient.query: Just thinking about doing something so simple makes me really anxious. I can almost see the text on my screen, and my mind races with all the things that could go wrong. I know it sounds silly, but it feels like a huge deal to me, and I don’t know how to break that down. It’s frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: all the things that could go wrong
it feels like a huge deal

llm_response.response: It’s understandable that even a simple task can feel overwhelming when your mind keeps listing potential mishaps. You’re not alone in feeling that the situation seems bigger than it is. One way to ease that pressure is to pause and focus on just one small step at a time, noticing how it feels when you actually start. This can help shift the sense of loss of control and reduce the anxiety that’s building up.

patient.query: I get what you're saying, and it makes sense. It’s just that even thinking about taking one small step feels like so much pressure. I want to try, but I often find myself stuck in that anxious loop, and then it’s hard to even start. I don’t know, it just feels really daunting.

triggers: thinking about taking one small step
automatic_thoughts: it feels like so much pressure
I often find myself stuck in that anxious loop

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that pressure feels, even for a tiny step. It’s understandable that the anxiety can trap you in a loop that makes starting feel impossible. Maybe we could try breaking the step into an even smaller, almost imperceptible action—just a single breath or a quick glance—so the task feels less daunting.

patient.query: I appreciate that suggestion. Honestly, even the idea of taking a single breath feels a bit overwhelming sometimes. It’s like I can see the goal, but when I think of actually moving toward it, my body just tightens up, and it’s hard to relax. I wish I could just feel more at ease with it all.

triggers: idea of taking a single breath
thinking of moving toward the goal
automatic_thoughts: wish I could feel more at ease

llm_response.response: I hear how the simple idea of a single breath can feel like a big, tight knot, and that tension makes it hard to relax. When that tightening happens, what does it feel like in your body? It might help to pause and notice something else—perhaps a sound, a texture, or a small movement—to give yourself a brief moment of ease.

patient.query: Honestly, even the idea of taking a single breath feels a bit overwhelming sometimes. It’s like I can see the goal, but when I think of actually moving toward it, my body just tightens up, and it’s hard to relax. I just wish I could feel more at ease with it all.

triggers: idea of taking a single breath
thinking of moving toward the goal
automatic_thoughts: I wish I could feel more at ease

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea of just one breath is bringing a lot of tension and overwhelm. When you notice that tightening, what does it feel like in your body? Are there moments when you can pause and notice any sensations that might help you feel a bit more grounded?
